data.customTitle: What should a PR review checklist include for an ML model...

inputState.inputText: What should a PR review checklist include for an ML model change?

message.text: What should a PR review checklist include for an ML model change?

response.value:
A PR review checklist for an ML model change must ensure standards compliance across code, documentation, testing, and MLOps.

What to do:
- Confirm all code follows PEP 8, uses type hints, and is in the correct project structure (§2.1, §2.3).
- Ensure public functions/classes have docstrings and complex logic is commented (§3.1).
- Check that experiments are logged in MLflow with parameters, metrics, artifacts, and Git SHA (§5.1).
- Verify a model card is present for production models (§3.3).
- Confirm ≥80% unit test coverage with pytest and that CI/CD checks pass (§4.3, §4.4).
- Ensure no secrets are in code/configs (§4.5).

Per §2.1, §3.1, §4.3, §4.4, §5.1, §3.3, and §4.5 of the Brambles DS Standards.

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Run Black and linters to enforce PEP 8 and type hints
- [ ] Add/verify docstrings and inline comments for all public code
- [ ] Log all experiments to MLflow with parameters, metrics, artifacts, and Git SHA
- [ ] Add or update a model card for the model
- [ ] Ensure ≥80% test coverage with pytest and CI/CD passing
- [ ] Scan code/configs for secrets and move them to approved secret managers

data.customTitle: How should we version control feature engineering logic?

inputState.inputText: How should we version control feature engineering logic?

message.text: How should we version control feature engineering logic?

response.value:
All feature engineering logic must be version controlled in GitHub or Bitbucket—never in local files or ad-hoc scripts. Store all code, configuration, and infrastructure-as-code in the repository. Use a branching strategy (e.g., Git Flow or trunk-based development), and ensure all changes go through pull requests with peer review. Commit messages should reference Jira ticket IDs and follow a consistent convention. Never hardcode credentials; use approved secrets management. Per §4.1 of the Brambles DS Standards.

What to do:
- Add all feature engineering scripts to src/project_name/features/ in your repo
- Commit changes with descriptive messages referencing Jira tickets
- Use feature branches for new logic
- Submit pull requests for review

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Add feature engineering code to src/project_name/features/
- [ ] Commit and push changes to GitHub/Bitbucket
- [ ] Create a feature branch (e.g., feature/JIRA-123-new-feature)
- [ ] Open a pull request for peer review
- [ ] Reference Jira ticket in commit messages and PR description

Per §4.1 of the Brambles DS Standards.

data.customTitle: What coding standards should DS team members follow for p...

inputState.inputText: What coding standards should DS team members follow for production code?

message.text: What coding standards should DS team members follow for production code?

response.value:
All DS team members must follow strict coding standards for production code:

- Use Python as the primary language and enforce PEP 8 style (choose 88 or 79 character line length and apply consistently).
- Use automated formatters and linters.
- Add type hints (PEP 484) to all production functions.
- Structure projects as specified (README.md, src/, tests/unit/, tests/integration/, notebooks/ for exploration only).
- Use snake_case for functions/variables, CamelCase for classes, ALL_CAPS for constants.
- Only use approved Gen AI tools (GitHub Copilot).
- All code must be version controlled in GitHub/Bitbucket.

Per §2.1–2.4, §4.1 of the Brambles DS Standards.

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Configure Black and flake8 in your repo for formatting and linting
- [ ] Add type hints to all production Python functions
- [ ] Organize your project using the required directory structure
- [ ] Enforce naming conventions in all code
- [ ] Use only GitHub Copilot for AI-assisted coding
- [ ] Commit all code to GitHub or Bitbucket

data.customTitle: What testing standards apply to data science code intende...

inputState.inputText: What testing standards apply to data science code intended for production?

message.text: What testing standards apply to data science code intended for production?

response.value:
All production data science code must have unit tests (≥80% coverage), integration tests for pipelines/endpoints, and data validation tests. Use pytest as the standard framework. Tests must run in CI/CD and pass before merging. Per §4.3 of the Brambles DS Standards.

What to do:
- Add pytest to requirements.txt and install it.
- Create tests/unit/ and tests/integration/ directories.
- Write test functions for all production code, targeting ≥80% coverage.
- Implement data validation tests using tools like Great Expectations or Deequ.
- Configure Bitbucket Pipelines to run all tests on every commit.

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Add pytest to requirements.txt and run pip install pytest
- [ ] Create tests/unit/ and tests/integration/ folders
- [ ] Write test functions for all production code (≥80% coverage)
- [ ] Add data validation tests (e.g., Great Expectations)
- [ ] Configure CI/CD to run all tests per §4.3 of the Brambles DS Standards

data.customTitle: How should we handle experiment tracking for hyperparamet...

inputState.inputText: How should we handle experiment tracking for hyperparameter tuning?

message.text: How should we handle experiment tracking for hyperparameter tuning?

response.value:
All hyperparameter tuning experiments must be logged to MLflow, capturing parameters, metrics, artifacts, and the source code version (Git SHA). This ensures full reproducibility and traceability of tuning runs. Always include the dataset version or reference for each run.

What to do:
- Log every hyperparameter tuning run to MLflow using mlflow.start_run().
- Record each hyperparameter with mlflow.log_param(), metrics with mlflow.log_metric(), and artifacts (e.g., model files) with mlflow.log_artifact().
- Tag each run with the Git SHA and dataset version.

Per §5.1 of the Brambles DS Standards.

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Set up MLflow tracking in your tuning script
- [ ] Log all hyperparameters, metrics, and artifacts for each run
- [ ] Tag each run with the Git SHA and dataset version for reproducibility

- [ ] Document performance, fairness, and limitations in the model card

data.customTitle: When is a model ready to move from development to staging?

inputState.inputText: When is a model ready to move from development to staging?

message.text: When is a model ready to move from development to staging?

response.value:
A model is ready to move from development to staging when it has passed a go/no-go checkpoint after exploration, all experiments are logged in MLflow with parameters, metrics, artifacts, and Git SHA, and the transition is coordinated with the Application Development team. Deployment to staging must include a model card, defined SLAs, and a rollback plan. Per §1.2 and §1.3 of the Brambles DS Standards.

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Log all experiments to MLflow with parameters, metrics, artifacts, and Git SHA
- [ ] Hold a go/no-go checkpoint and document the decision
- [ ] Prepare a model card, define SLAs, and create a rollback plan
- [ ] Collaborate with Application Development to transition to staging

message.text: How should we compare a candidate model against the current production baseline?

response.value:

Register both models in the Databricks/MLflow Model Registry, then evaluate them on the same dataset and compare performance metrics. Use canary or shadow deployments for high-risk changes. Document results in a model card.

Per §5.1 and §5.2 of the Brambles DS Standards.

**To action this immediately:**

- [ ] Register both models in the Databricks/MLflow Model Registry
- [ ] Evaluate both models on the same dataset and compare metrics
- [ ] Document results and update the model card

data.customTitle: How should secrets, credentials, and configuration be man...

inputState.inputText: How should secrets, credentials, and configuration be managed in data science code and pipelines?

message.text: How should secrets, credentials, and configuration be managed in data science code and pipelines?

response.value:
All secrets, credentials, and sensitive configuration must never be hardcoded in code or config files. Instead, store all sensitive values in AWS Secrets Manager, AWS SSM Parameter Store, Bitbucket Repository Variables, or Databricks Secrets. Environment-specific config should be managed via environment variables or config files that are excluded from version control (e.g., in .gitignore). Per §4.5 of the Brambles DS Standards.

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Move all credentials and API keys to AWS Secrets Manager, SSM Parameter Store, Bitbucket Repository Variables, or Databricks Secrets
- [ ] Reference secrets in code via environment variables, not hardcoded values
- [ ] Add sensitive config files to .gitignore to prevent version control inclusion

data.customTitle: How should we handle model rollback if prediction quality...

inputState.inputText: How should we handle model rollback if prediction quality degrades?

message.text: How should we handle model rollback if prediction quality degrades?

response.value:
To handle model rollback if prediction quality degrades, you must define and implement a rollback plan as part of your deployment process. This plan should enable you to revert to a previous, validated model version using the Databricks/MLflow Model Registry and your deployment platform (e.g., Databricks Model Serving, ECS/EKS). Per §1.3 and §5.2 of the Brambles DS Standards, rollback procedures are mandatory for all production deployments.

**What to do:**
1. Define rollback criteria (e.g., performance thresholds, drift alerts) during deployment.
2. Register all model versions in the MLflow Model Registry.
3. Use the registry to promote a previous model version to "Production" if quality degrades.
4. Document rollback steps in your runbook and ensure the Support team is trained.

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Define rollback triggers and document them in your deployment plan and model card
- [ ] Register all model versions in the Databricks/MLflow Model Registry
- [ ] Implement and test rollback by promoting a previous model version to "Production" in the registry
- [ ] Update runbooks with step-by-step rollback instructions for the Support team

Per §1.3 and §5.2 of the Brambles DS Standards.

response.value:

For general projects, implement data validation tests using tools like Great Expectations or Deequ to ensure input data quality (§4.3). **To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Add Great Expectations or Deequ to your project for data validation tests
- [ ] Create and version labeling guidelines and annotation schemas for CV projects
- [ ] Implement automated annotation quality checks and dataset versioning

data.customTitle: What ethical review steps are required before deploying a...

inputState.inputText: What ethical review steps are required before deploying a model that affects people?

message.text: What ethical review steps are required before deploying a model that affects people?

response.value:
Before deploying any model that affects people, you must conduct an ethical review focused on fairness, bias, privacy, and intended use. For computer vision or models handling images of people, you must complete a privacy impact assessment, implement PII detection/redaction (e.g., face blurring), and document potential biases in training data. Every production model must have a model card covering fairness, bias, ethical considerations, intended use, and limitations.

Per §3.3 and §7.5 of the Brambles DS Standards.

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Complete a privacy impact assessment for models affecting people
- [ ] Implement PII detection and redaction in data pipelines
- [ ] Document fairness, bias, and ethical considerations in the model card

data.customTitle: What is the role of Jira in the data science workflow?

inputState.inputText: What is the role of Jira in the data science workflow?

message.text: What is the role of Jira in the data science workflow?

response.value:
Jira is the single system of record for all project management in the data science workflow. It is used to track project charters, epics, stories, bugs, spikes, and production incidents. All exploratory work, project initiation, and incident tracking must be documented and managed in Jira to ensure transparency and accountability.

**What to do:**
- Create and maintain a project charter in Jira for every new project.
- Track all exploratory tasks, development work, and incidents as Jira issues (epics, stories, bugs, spikes).
- Update Jira regularly to reflect project status and progress.

Per §1.1 and §1.6 of the Brambles DS Standards.

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Create a Jira project and add a project charter with business problem, approach, and KPIs
- [ ] Log all tasks, experiments, and incidents as Jira issues
- [ ] Update and review Jira issues throughout the project lifecycle

data.customTitle: What are the requirements for designing an A/B test?

inputState.inputText: What are the requirements for designing an A/B test?

message.text: What are the requirements for designing an A/B test?

response.value:
To design an A/B test, you must:

1. Pre-register hypotheses before data collection—no post-hoc hypothesis formulation.
2. Perform a power analysis and sample size calculation (minimum 80% power at α = 0.05).
3. Undergo peer review of the experiment design by at least one other data scientist (senior/staff review for high-stakes tests).
4. Clearly distinguish between correlational and causal claims, using causal language only with valid methodology.

Per §1.5 of the Brambles DS Standards.

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Pre-register your hypotheses in a documented form before collecting data
- [ ] Calculate required sample size and power (≥80% at α = 0.05) using a statistical tool
- [ ] Submit your experiment design for peer review (and senior review if high-stakes)
- [ ] Document the methodology and claim type (correlational/causal)

data.customTitle: What is the standard project directory structure for a DS...

inputState.inputText: What is the standard project directory structure for a DS repository?

message.text: What is the standard project directory structure for a DS repository?

response.value:
The standard project directory structure for a DS repository is:

project-name/
├── README.md  
├── setup.py / pyproject.toml  
├── src/project_name/  
│   ├── data/, features/, models/, utils/  
├── tests/  
│   ├── unit/  
│   └── integration/  
├── notebooks/        ← exploratory only, never production  
├── configs/  
├── docs/  
└── requirements.txt / environment.yml

Notebooks are for exploration only; production logic must be in .py files under src/. Per §2.3 of the Brambles DS Standards.

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Create the directory structure above in your repository
- [ ] Place exploratory notebooks in notebooks/ only
- [ ] Refactor production code into src/project_name/
- [ ] Add README.md, requirements.txt, and setup.py or pyproject.toml

data.customTitle: What ethical and privacy requirements apply to CV project...

inputState.inputText: What ethical and privacy requirements apply to CV projects involving images of people?

message.text: What ethical and privacy requirements apply to CV projects involving images of people?

response.value:
All computer vision (CV) projects involving images of people must meet strict ethical and privacy requirements:

1. Conduct a privacy impact assessment for the project.
2. Implement PII detection and redaction in data pipelines—this includes face blurring and license plate obfuscation where required.
3. Document potential biases in training data, such as demographic representation and geographic coverage.

Per §7.5 of the Brambles DS Standards, these steps are mandatory for all CV projects handling images of people.

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Conduct and document a privacy impact assessment for your CV project
- [ ] Implement PII detection and redaction (e.g., face blurring) in your data pipeline
- [ ] Document all potential biases in your training data

message.text: What are the Tier 1 no-regret moves the team should implement immediately?

The Tier 1 no-regret moves are the foundational actions every team should implement immediately to align with the Brambles DS Standards:

1. Set up all code, configs, and infra in GitHub/Bitbucket with a clear branching strategy (§4.1).
2. Enforce automated linting, formatting, and unit tests (≥80% coverage) using pytest and CI/CD pipelines (§2.1, §4.3, §4.4).
3. Log all experiments in MLflow with parameters, metrics, artifacts, and Git SHA (§5.1).
4. Never hardcode secrets—use AWS Secrets Manager, SSM, Bitbucket Variables, or Databricks Secrets (§4.5).
5. Ensure every repo has a comprehensive README.md (§3.1).

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Migrate all code/config to GitHub or Bitbucket and set up main/develop/feature branches
- [ ] Add and enforce Black, flake8, and pytest in CI/CD (Bitbucket Pipelines)
- [ ] Integrate MLflow logging in all experiment scripts and tag runs with Git SHA
- [ ] Move all secrets to AWS Secrets Manager, SSM, Bitbucket Variables, or Databricks Secrets
- [ ] Create/update README.md with purpose, setup, usage, architecture, and contacts

Per §2.1, §3.1, §4.1, §4.3, §4.4, §4.5, and §5.1 of the Brambles DS Standards.

message.text: What does the model registry promotion workflow look like end to end?

response.value:
The model registry promotion workflow is managed in the Databricks/MLflow Model Registry and follows a staged approval process: models move from Staging → Production → Archived, with approval required for each transition. This ensures only validated models are promoted to production.

**What to do:**
1. Register your trained model in the Databricks/MLflow Model Registry.
2. Assign the model to the "Staging" stage for testing and validation.
3. Submit the model for review and approval before promoting to "Production."
4. Move the model to "Production" only after approval.
5. Archive older or deprecated models as needed.

Per §5.2 of the Brambles DS Standards.

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Register your model in the Databricks/MLflow Model Registry
- [ ] Assign the model to "Staging" and validate it
- [ ] Submit for approval and promote to "Production" after review
- [ ] Archive outdated models as required

data.customTitle: How should model retraining be triggered and managed?

inputState.inputText: How should model retraining be triggered and managed?

message.text: How should model retraining be triggered and managed?

response.value:
Model retraining must be triggered either on a scheduled cadence or automatically when data or model performance drift is detected. Retraining pipelines must be automated, but require human-in-the-loop approval before promoting new models to production. Production predictions and outcomes must be captured to enable feedback loops for continuous improvement. Per §5.5 of the Brambles DS Standards.

**What to do:**
- Define retraining triggers (scheduled or drift-based) at deployment.
- Implement automated retraining pipelines with approval gates.
- Capture and store production predictions and outcomes for feedback.

**To action this immediately:**
- [ ] Configure drift detection using Evidently AI, CloudWatch, or Databricks Lakehouse Monitoring
- [ ] Set up automated retraining pipelines with human approval before production promotion
- [ ] Log production predictions and outcomes to enable feedback loops per §5.5
